message.content:
Try HARD to REFUTE this proposed lever for improving a RACE score. Default to refuted=true when uncertain.

LEVER: Synthesis layer: an 'argue' pass exempt from span-grounding
TARGETS: insight (0.32) / Critical Synthesis and Nuanced Evaluation w=0.25; Emergent Themes / Theoretical Linkages w=0.20; Analytical Depth in Mechanisms w=0.25 (weight 0.32)
CHANGE: Add a compose-stage pass AFTER facts are assembled per section: for each section, generate 2-3 interpretive paragraphs that (a) reconcile conflicting numbers already in the section (e.g. our body already contains Humlum's precise-null Danish results [95] next to 15% RCT gains [42] and never says the obvious thing the reference says at its line 100 about lags and scale), (b) state which pattern the evidence supports and where it disagrees, (c) tie back to the displacement/reinstatement lens. Verification rule for these paragraphs: NOT span-grounding but entailment — every interpretive claim must be entailed by ≥2 already-verified facts it cites. Also generate one thesis paragraph in the intro (the reference's 'promise and peril' duality move) and rewrite the conclusion as consensus → disagreements → named research gaps, each bullet derived from tensions the argue pass surfaced.
CLAIMED GAIN: +0.020 to +0.030 (insight 0.424 → ~0.49; the reference's ~13 citation-free interpretive paragraphs are the single largest measured difference between the texts)
CLAIMED FAILURE MODE: Three ways: (1) the model writes generic hedged filler ('the evidence is mixed') that the judge reads as padding — enforce that each paragraph must name ≥2 specific cited findings and take a position on their relationship; (2) entailment-checking is weaker than span-verify, so an over-claim slips through and costs credibility; (3) at fixed 9k words the synthesis displaces facts and comprehensiveness dips — pay for it out of the padding cut (next lever), not out of coverage.

ATTACK IT THROUGH THIS LENS:
BACKFIRE: could this lever LOWER another dimension? Depth levers already cost us readability. Weights: insight .32, comprehensiveness .29, instruction_following .25, readability .14.

Context: our report /home/polaris/wt/flywheel/outputs/rank10_sections_compose/report.md scored 0.4313. Reference /home/polaris/polaris_project/RACE_REFERENCE_task72.md = 0.5000 by definition. SOTA = 0.5265.
Read the files if you need to check a claim. Be adversarial, concrete, and brief.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

# POLARIS BENCHMARK TARGET — the real scoreboard (from web search 2026-07-11)

## What we must beat
**FS-Researcher** (arXiv:2602.01566, ACL 2026) — "Test-Time Scaling for Long-Horizon Research
Tasks with File-System-Based Agents". A file-system dual-agent (Context Builder + Report Writer)
that scales research BEYOND the context window via a persistent knowledge base. This is the same
thesis as POLARIS (process ALL 329 baskets (current s2s3 iter-7 count; 346 was the stale iter-6 count), not one context window). It claims **SOTA report
quality** across backbones. Code: github.com/Ignoramus0817/FS-Researcher.

## The benchmarks it is scored on (both open-ended report quality)
1. **DeepResearch Bench** (arXiv:2506.11763) — 100 PhD-level research tasks.
   - Scorer: **RACE** — reference-based LLM judge, 4 dimensions with per-task DYNAMIC weights:
     Comprehensiveness, Insight/Depth, Instruction-Following, Readability.
     Compares the target report against a high-quality REFERENCE report.
   - Download: `github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench` — tasks at
     `data/prompt_data/query.jsonl` (100 tasks); HF dataset `muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Dataset`.
   - Leaderboard (ChatGPT/Gemini deep-research RACE scores) is on HuggingFace.
2. **DeepConsult** — open-ended consulting-style report quality (pairwise/quality judge).
3. (also referenced for the agent side: BrowseComp, BFCL, TAU-Bench, MCP Agent Bench.)

Related: **DeepResearch Bench II** (arXiv:2601.08536) — rubric-from-expert-report diagnosis
(the DRB-II wrapper already stubbed in the compete wheel).

## What this means for POLARIS
- **RACE is the correct instrument** — confirms the compete wheel's direction; DeepTRACE (faithfulness)
  is a SEPARATE, complementary axis, not the mission's quality axis.
- "Beat FS-Researcher" is now RUNNABLE: run POLARIS on the DeepResearch Bench 100 tasks, score with
  RACE against the reference reports, compare to FS-Researcher + the ChatGPT/Gemini leaderboard.
- The two build wheels serve this end:
  - **OUTLINE** → depth + compute-and-prove drives Comprehensiveness + Insight (RACE dims 1-2) and
    the faithfulness moat (DeepTRACE) that frontier products lack.
  - **COMPOSE** → renders all 329 baskets (current s2s3 iter-7 count; 346 was the stale iter-6 count) fast + faithful so nothing is dropped for latency =
    Comprehensiveness + Readability, at full corpus depth.
- Open gap: we still have NO FS-Researcher report artifact locally; the benchmark download closes it
  (we can run POLARIS on the same 100 tasks and score head-to-head).

content.content:
TASK 72 PROMPT:
Please write a literature review on the restructuring impact of Artificial Intelligence (AI) on the labor market. Focus on how AI, as a key driver of the Fourth Industrial Revolution, is causing significant disruptions and affecting various industries. Ensure the review only cites high-quality, English-language journal articles.

DIMENSION WEIGHTS: {"readability": 0.14, "insight": 0.32, "comprehensiveness": 0.29, "instruction_following": 0.25}

=== COMPREHENSIVENESS (weight 0.29) ===
- [w=0.10] Grounding in AI and the Fourth Industrial Revolution (4IR) Context :: Assesses whether the review adequately defines AI within the context of the 4IR and explains its fundamental role as a driver of labor market restructuring. This contextual framing is crucial for understanding the scope and nature of the impacts discussed.
- [w=0.25] Breadth of Labor Market Restructuring Dimensions Covered :: Evaluates the extent to which the review addresses the multifaceted nature of AI's impact on the labor market, including job creation, job displacement, job transformation, changes in skill demands, wage effects, and productivity changes.
- [w=0.25] Scope of Industry-Specific Analysis :: Checks if the review examines the restructuring impact of AI across a diverse range of relevant industries, highlighting both common trends and sector-specific nuances. The task explicitly requires focusing on 'various industries.'
- [w=0.15] Exploration of AI's Disruptive Character and Scale :: Assesses whether the review sufficiently explores the 'significant disruptions' caused by AI, including the magnitude, speed, and transformative potential of these changes on labor market structures and dynamics.
- [w=0.15] Depth and Representativeness of Literature Synthesized :: Evaluates if the review synthesizes a broad and representative selection of current, high-quality academic literature (from the specified journal articles), covering the main research themes, findings, and ongoing debates regarding AI's impact on the labor market. This ensures the review's content is built upon a solid and comprehensive foundation of existing knowledge.
- [w=0.10] Balanced Discussion of AI's Labor Market Impacts :: Assesses whether the review presents a nuanced perspective by discussing both the challenges (e.g., displacement, skill gaps, inequality) and opportunities (e.g., new job creation, productivity gains, enhanced work quality) arising from AI's influence on the labor market.

=== INSIGHT (weight 0.32) ===
- [w=0.25] Analytical Depth in Characterizing AI-Driven Labor Market Restructuring Mechanisms :: Assesses if the review moves beyond superficial descriptions of labor market changes to deeply analyze the underlying *mechanisms* (e.g., task automation, skill augmentation, job creation/destruction dynamics, organizational adaptation) through which AI specifically reshapes job roles, skill requirements, and overall labor market structures.
- [w=0.25] Critical Synthesis and Nuanced Evaluation of AI's Disruptive Impacts Across Industries :: Evaluates the review's ability to critically synthesize diverse findings from high-quality journal articles to present a nuanced understanding of AI's 'significant disruptions.' This includes identifying patterns of impact, variations across different industries, and areas of consensus, debate, or uncertainty in the literature, rather than merely cataloging effects.
- [w=0.15] Insightful Integration of AI's Role within the Fourth Industrial Revolution (4IR) Context :: Measures how effectively the review articulates and leverages the 4IR framework to provide a deeper, more contextualized understanding of the *nature, scale, and interconnectedness* of AI's transformative power on the labor market, rather than treating AI's impact in isolation or merely mentioning the 4IR superficially.
- [w=0.20] Identification and Articulation of Emergent Themes, Theoretical Linkages, or Novel Perspectives :: Assesses whether the review transcends simple summarization to identify and clearly articulate overarching emergent themes, significant theoretical linkages between concepts, or potentially novel perspectives derived from the synthesis of the reviewed literature on AI's labor market impact.
- [w=0.15] Value and Foresight in Delineating Implications and Future Research Agendas :: Evaluates the review's capacity to distill strategically valuable implications (e.g., for policy, education, workforce adaptation strategies) and to clearly delineate critical research gaps or promising future research agendas based on the comprehensive synthesis of the current state of knowledge.

=== INSTRUCTION_FOLLOWING (weight 0.25) ===
- [w=0.10] Adherence to 'Literature Review' Format and Purpose :: Assesses if the article is structured and executed as a literature review, meaning it synthesizes and discusses existing published research on the topic, rather than presenting new empirical findings or being solely an opinion piece.
- [w=0.20] Consistent Focus on 'Restructuring Impact of AI on the Labor Market' :: Evaluates whether the article's content remains consistently and primarily focused on AI's restructuring effects on the labor market, as per the core task instruction, without significant digressions into unrelated AI applications or general economic theory not tied to labor impacts.
- [w=0.15] Integration of 'AI as a Key Driver of the Fourth Industrial Revolution' Theme :: Checks if the review explicitly incorporates and discusses AI within the specific context of the Fourth Industrial Revolution, as mandated by the task's focus requirements.
- [w=0.15] Explicit Addressal of AI-Driven 'Significant Disruptions' in the Labor Market :: Assesses whether the review specifically addresses and elaborates on the 'significant disruptions' caused by AI in the labor market, fulfilling another key focus point of the task.
- [w=0.15] Coverage of AI's Impact on 'Various Industries' :: Evaluates if the review discusses the impact of AI on the labor market across a spectrum of 'various industries,' rather than limiting its scope to a single industry or providing only a generic overview, as per the instruction.
- [w=0.15] Exclusive Citation of 'High-Quality Journal Articles' :: Verifies that all cited sources are academic, peer-reviewed journal articles (or meet a clear standard of 'high-quality' if defined contextually, e.g., leading journals), and that other forms of literature (e.g., books, conference proceedings, news articles, blogs, non-peer-reviewed reports) are not cited, adhering to the 'only cites high-quality... journal articles' constraint.
- [w=0.10] Exclusive Citation of 'English-Language' Journal Articles :: Ensures that all journal articles cited in the review are originally published in English or are established English translations, strictly adhering to the language constraint for sources.

=== READABILITY (weight 0.14) ===
- [w=0.20] L1: Language Clarity, Precision, and Academic Tone :: Evaluates the accuracy and fluency of sentence construction, grammar, spelling, and punctuation. Assesses the precise, consistent, and appropriate use of technical terminology related to AI, labor markets, the Fourth Industrial Revolution, and specific industries. Ensures an objective, formal academic tone suitable for a literature review. For example, terms like 'skill-biased technical change' or specific AI techniques should be used correctly and, if necessary, briefly contextualized for a broader academic audience.
- [w=0.20] S1: Overall Structure and Logical Organization :: Assesses the coherence and logic of the review's macro-structure. This includes a clear introduction defining the review's scope, purpose, and organizational map; logically sequenced thematic sections with informative headings that reflect the facets of AI's impact on labor (e.g., by industry, skill level, type of disruption); and a conclusion that effectively synthesizes the main themes from the literature and perhaps points to gaps or future research directions.
- [w=0.15] S2: Paragraph Cohesion and Transitions :: Evaluates if each paragraph focuses on a single, clear idea or theme derived from the literature, with sentences flowing logically within the paragraph. Assesses the effectiveness of transitional phrases, sentences, and logical connectors in linking ideas smoothly between paragraphs and across different sections of the review, ensuring a coherent narrative thread.
- [w=0.15] P1: Clarity and Synthesis in Presenting Sourced Information :: Assesses how clearly, concisely, and accurately key arguments, findings, theories, and methodologies from the cited journal articles are presented. Evaluates the effectiveness of synthesizing information from multiple sources to build a coherent understanding of topics (e.g., AI's effect on job displacement vs. creation, or changes in skill demands across industries), rather than just serially summarizing individual papers. Checks for appropriate information density and avoidance of redundancy.
- [w=0.10] D1: Clarity of Data/Evidence Referenced or Summarized :: Evaluates the clarity with which quantitative or qualitative data, empirical findings, or specific evidence from cited studies (e.g., statistics on automation rates, survey results on worker sentiment) are described, interpreted, or summarized within the review. If the review itself uses tables or figures to compile or synthesize information from sources (e.g., a summary table of key studies and their findings), assesses their clarity, labeling, and effectiveness in aiding comprehension.
- [w=0.10] F1: Formatting, Layout, and Visual Consistency :: Evaluates the use of consistent and professional formatting (e.g., font choice and size, spacing, margins, heading styles) that enhances readability. Assesses the overall visual layout for ease of navigation and reduced reader fatigue, including clear paragraph breaks. Also considers the visual consistency and unobtrusive integration of in-text citations and the clarity of the bibliography's formatting, which contributes to the academic presentation.
- [w=0.10] A1: Audience Adaptation and Explanation of Terms :: Assesses if the language, level of detail, and style of argumentation are appropriate for an academic audience interested in AI's impact on the labor market. Evaluates whether potentially ambiguous or highly specialized terms are sufficiently contextualized or briefly explained to ensure understanding by readers who may not be experts in every specific sub-domain discussed (e.g., specific AI algorithms or niche economic theories).
====
Please write a literature review on the restructuring impact of Artificial Intelligence (AI) on the labor market. Focus on how AI, as a key driver of the Fourth Industrial Revolution, is causing significant disruptions and affecting various industries. Ensure the review only cites high-quality, English-language journal articles.

# A literature review on the restructuring impact of Artificial Intelligence (AI) on the labor market

This report synthesizes the retrieved research evidence on the question above. It is organized as a coherent review: an introduction that frames the scope, thematic sections that group the evidence by sub-topic, a cross-study synthesis that surfaces where the findings agree and conflict, and a closing discussion of conclusions and open research gaps. Every quantitative claim is span-grounded to a cited source; claims that could not be verified against the underlying evidence were removed rather than paraphrased.

## Introduction and Scope of the Evidence

Within this framework, automation generates a displacement effect that reduces labor's share of value added, countered by a reinstatement effect as new tasks reinstate labor into a broader range of activities, and the net impact depends on the relative magnitudes of these opposing forces.[1] The contemporary context for these dynamics is the Fourth Industrial Revolution, characterized by a fusion of technologies blurring the lines between the physical, digital, and biological spheres and evolving at an exponential rather than linear pace.[2] Within this revolution, AI and smart robotics are de

## Theoretical Foundations: Task-Based Models of Automation and Reinstatement

This framework, building on prior contributions including Autor, Levy, and Murnane (2003), Acemoglu and Autor (2011), and Zeira (1998), posits that production requires tasks allocated to either capital or labor, and that technological change alters the task content of production rather than simply augmenting factor productivity.[1][13] (also mirrored) Within this framework, automation corresponds to the development of technologies enabling capital to substitute for labor in specific tasks, generating what the authors term a "displacement effect" that reduces labor's share of value added.[1] (a

## Occupational Exposure to AI: Measures and Susceptibility

Early foundational work by Frey and Osborne estimated the probability of computerisation for 702 detailed occupations using a Gaussian process classifier, establishing a task-based exposure methodology that became dominant in the field of AI labor market research.[24][25] Building on this paradigm, Felten, Raj, and Seamans developed a novel dataset measuring occupational, industry, and geographic exposure to artificial intelligence, published in the Strategic Management Journal.[26] Their approach, unlike the original Frey and Osborne framework, allowed temporal updating but still did not dist

## Generative AI: Early Empirical Evidence on Employment and Productivity

The study also documents a 1.2 percentage point decrease in the probability of receiving any employment in a given month—approximately a 10% drop relative to baseline—and a 4.7% reduction in the number of jobs conditional on being employed.[38] By contrast, aggregate labor-market indicators through 2024–2025 show limited economywide disruption; one analysis reports that 35.9% of U.S. workers used generative AI by December 2025 but finds only small positive wage effects and no statistically significant declines in job openings or employment in exposed occupations.[39] A prominent Harvard 

## AI, Skills, and Task Transformation

A systematic review of AI's impact on employment and skill demand finds that the effects are multifaceted, with new high-skilled jobs and rising demand for interdisciplinary talent emerging alongside the replacement of low-skilled jobs and polarization of the employment structure.[46] This review further notes that AI adoption necessitates a balanced approach to developing both hard and soft skills, ensuring individuals can effectively collaborate with and leverage AI technologies.[47] Gaps in digital skills, critical thinking, and self-management hinder AI adoption, with financial resources, 

## Distributional Impacts: Inequality, Job Quality, and the Digital Divide

A joint analysis by the Brookings Institution and the Stanford Digital Economy Lab finds that over 30 percent of U.S. workers could see at least half of their job tasks affected by generative AI, with exposure concentrated in white-collar and entry-level occupations rather than in traditional manufacturing or manual labor.[58] One comprehensive displacement analysis projects that 85 million jobs will be displaced by 2025, while 97 million new roles will simultaneously emerge, representing a net positive job creation of 12 million positions globally.[59] However, the gendered dimension of this 

## Policy Responses to AI-Driven Labor Market Restructuring

In the United States, the House announced a bipartisan task force on AI in February 2024, reflecting growing congressional interest in the economic impacts of the technology.[64] Survey data from February 2024 show that 84.6% of businesses reporting a "small number" of tasks replaced by AI declined to 79.2% six months later, while those reporting a "moderate number" rose from 13.0% to 17.7%, and those reporting a "large number" increased from 2.4% to 3.1%.[64] Employment effects remained largely unchanged in the near term, with 94.6% of firms reporting "no change" in employment due to AI, decl

## Cross-Study Synthesis and Contradictions

Bureau of Labor Statistics data or projections for the idea of a general acceleration of job loss or a structural break with trends pre-dating the AI revolution.[70] Using more conservative specifications with agent and tenure fixed effects, the deployment of AI increased resolutions per hour by 0.30 chats, or 13.8%, from an average of 2.12.[71] (also mirrored) The study also documented a 3.8 minute decrease in average customer chat duration, a 9% decline from the baseline mean, and a 0.37 unit increase in chats handled per hour, representing roughly a 14% increase relative to a baseline mean 

## Conclusions and Research Gaps

Early research findings focusing on how AI affects labor demand are collectively inconsistent, and any findings based on current data are necessarily weak signals about the future.[82] Multiple studies using the Current Population Survey—including work by Gimbel et al. (2025), Eckhardt and Goldschlag (2025), and Dominski and Lee (2025)—show at most small changes in hiring in AI-exposed jobs.[84] Separately, Frank et al. (2026) found similar results, and some analyses argue that the timing of declines in job postings corresponds better to rising interest rates than to the launch of large la

## Additional Corroborated Findings

By contrast, a more cautious task-based framework by Acemoglu et al. estimates that AI's contribution to total factor productivity gains may not exceed 0.7 percentage points over ten years even under full automation of exposed tasks, with GDP impact remaining modest at approximately 1.1%.[93] IMF simulations by Cerutti et al. illustrate two contrasting trajectories: a rapid adoption scenario in which global TFP grows by 2.4% over ten years and global GDP increases by 4%, versus a slower scenario with TFP growth of only 1.8% and GDP growth limited to approximately 1.3%.[93] Census Bureau study 

## Limitations

Limitations: The corpus skews heavily toward secondary and synthesized sources, with only 6% of materials classified as T1 primary studies, while T4 and T6 tiers each account for 21% and a further 15% of sources remain of UNKNOWN tier, indicating substantial gaps in high-certainty empirical coverage. No contradictions were detected by the pipeline, so no cross-source disagreement on magnitude, direction, or endpoint is asserted. The telemetry block does not surface an explicit date range for the corpus, which limits the ability to characterize the evidence horizon or identify temporal gaps in

38:- 2026-07-12 | Rank5 outline CRASHED — **not my code** | After the judge succeeded, the run died in the OUTLINE agent: `ReasoningFirstTruncationError` — the reasoning-first outline model (deepseek-v4-pro) overran its budget and the LLM client **refused to promote truncated reasoning as content** ("Promoting truncated reasoning-derived text as verified prose would ship the model's scratchpad"). That is a pre-existing **fail-loud safety guard doing its job**, not a regression: the control run never tripped it, the judge verdict was fully captured, and nothing was corrupted. Retrying with `PG_OUTLINE_REASONING_MAX_TOKENS=49152` / `PG_OUTLINE_MIN_MAX_TOKENS=24576`. **Deliberately did NOT add a judge-verdict cache** to avoid re-paying the ~13min judge on retry — a stale cached verdict is exactly the failure class §-1.3.1 forbids, and saving 13 minutes is not worth reintroducing it.
45:**52 failed fetches (Cloudflare/ResearchGate challenge cards) were grounding report prose as citable T7 "sources"; they are now deleted and disclosed.** The faithfulness engine could never have caught this — strict_verify checks sentence↔span fidelity, and a sentence *can* be entailed by the text of a challenge card, so chrome passed the only hard gate and still grounded prose on nothing.
51:- Open quality gap (still unaddressed, from the laptop handoff): verified MATH/STAT synthesis (the outliner collects numbers but does not compute them), and report length ~4k words vs competitors' 10-22k.
57:- 2026-07-12 | THE MISSING MEASUREMENT (what Fable's ceiling argument does NOT settle) | Every compose measurement this project has ever taken — including the `kept_fraction=0.35` collapse that proves "more rows => thinner prose" — was taken on the **DIRTY corpus, the one that was ~50% alien**. The writer's menu held job listings, paralegal reading lists and Cloudflare captchas; it wrote sentences from them; strict_verify correctly killed them. **That is what kept_fraction=0.35 IS.** Rank5 removed precisely those rows — and **we have never once composed a report from the clean pool** (Rank5 stopped at outline-only). So the ceiling's own load-bearing number is confounded: on clean evidence the same fixed 10-18-sentence target should SURVIVE verification at a far higher rate, i.e. more verified words from the same prompt. Composing the clean pool is the cheapest experiment that can distinguish "compose is a hard architectural ceiling" from "compose was being fed garbage", and it must be run BEFORE any compose-stage change is designed. Launching it now.
62:- 2026-07-12 21:40 | SECOND FINDING (independent of the A/B): **TIER IS NOT RELEVANCE — a tier-based "quality firewall" would be actively harmful.** 56 of the dump bucket's rows are T1/T2 (highest credibility) and flagly off-topic: a gradeless-learning RCT (T1), a mass-deworming study (T1), Wiley's *Regulation & Governance* (T2), a Chicago Quantitative Alliance reading list (T2). The codebase already knows this (compose_agentic_report_s3gear329.py:155-160: *"tier is NOT a keep-veto ... a blanket high-tier exemption would shield precisely the credible-tier junk this audit found"*). **This retires the operator's proposed experiment #1 (SOURCE-ELIGIBILITY FIREWALL on tier/peer-reviewed/English) as originally specified: gating on tier-a prior would have KEPT all 56 of those rows and deleted nothing that matters.** Relevance, not credibility, is the axis that is broken — and the two-pass semantic judge already on the bench is the instrument for it. Structural depth gap also logged: the synthesis section ("Cross-Study Synthesis: Convergences and Contradictions"), the single most RACE-depth-bearing section, is STARVED at 14 rows while the catch-all holds 545.
63:- 2026-07-12 21:50 | **FABLE GATE: LOCK A *REFUTED* — MY CONFOUND THESIS WAS WRONG, AND THE REAL CEILING IS NOW NAMED.** I argued the ~51%-alien menu was what strict_verify was killing (=> kept_fraction 0.35, => clean pool would grow the report for free). **strict_verify is TOPICALITY-BLIND.** `strict_verify(draft_text, evidence_pool)` (provenance_generator.py:3598) never takes the research question, and its NLI judge is `judge(self, sentence, span)` (entailment_judge.py:740) prompted *"Decide whether the SPAN entails the SENTENCE"* (:584) — no question, no topic, no section title. **A sentence faithfully written from a real-but-ALIEN source is entailed by its span and is KEPT.** Off-topic evidence therefore produces kept-but-IRRELEVANT sentences, never dropped ones — so it cannot be what kept_fraction measures. (Verified all 3 claims myself against source.) The clean-pool A/B still running is now a FALSIFICATION TEST of Fable's prediction: armed kept_fraction should move <=±0.05. Its real payoff was never length — it is the RELEVANCE of shipped prose, i.e. a RACE-score win.
64:- 2026-07-12 21:50 | **THE ACTUAL CEILING (Fable, verified): THERE IS NO LENGTH GOVERNOR ON THIS PATH AT ALL.** `PG_TARGET_TOTAL_WORDS=8000`, `PG_SYNTHESIS_MAX_EXPANSION_PASSES`, `PG_MAX_WORDS_PER_SECTION`, `PG_SECTION_TOKEN_BUDGET`, `PG_SECTION_WRITER_MAX_TOKENS` are consumed ONLY by `agents/synthesizer.py` + `synthesis/section_writer.py`, reached via graph.py:1995 -> synthesize_report. **`multi_section_generator.py` imports NEITHER (grep: 0 hits)** and compose_agentic_report_s3gear329.py:594 calls `generate_multi_section_report` directly. Every word-budget knob in .env is DEAD CODE on the flywheel path; :11646 only *sums* total_words as telemetry. **Report length is purely emergent: n_sections x rule-#8 sentence target x ~30 w/sentence, minus B11 dedup.** Measured on control_report.md (3875w): evidence-rich sections already render 16-18 sentences = AT the top of rule #8's band, i.e. **the target is binding exactly where evidence is rich.** Corrected ceiling ~9 x 18 x 30 = 4.9k. Frontier = 10-22k. Section count is NOT the bound (facet outline allows 40; we use 9).
74:- 2026-07-12 22:15 | **THE RELEVANCE PAYOFF IS ~ZERO — MY OWN 21:40 THESIS IS DEAD.** I argued (and Fable half-endorsed) that a ~51%-alien menu poisons shipped prose, so the clean pool's prize is RELEVANCE, i.e. a RACE win. **Measured it instead of assuming: the dirty report's shipped bibliography is 1.6% alien — ONE row (ev_852) out of 63.** The armed report: 0 of 72. **A ~51%-alien POOL yields a ~1.6%-alien BIBLIOGRAPHY** — the section writer was already discarding alien rows at citation time. So the relevance prize is worth *one citation*. Both the "poisoned prose" story and the RACE-relevance payoff are **refuted by the data**, and they were mine.
82:- 2026-07-12 22:30 | FABLE ON CLAIM 1 (relevance ~zero): **CONFIRMED — independently recomputed from raw files, 1.6% stands.** But **two latent holes in my method that happened not to bite**, and they must be on the record: (i) my `ev_\d+` regex over report.md was **structurally vacuous** — prose cites with `[n]` markers, and both runs record `leaked_cite_ev_tokens: 0`, so that leg could never have matched anything; (ii) corpus ids are **zero-padded** (`ev_050`), so a raw string intersection silently misses unpadded variants. The answer survived only because the bibliography `evidence_id` leg carried it. **A measurement that returns the right answer through a dead code path is not a measurement.**
83:- 2026-07-12 22:30 | **NEW DEFECT, FOUND BY FABLE, SHIPPED IN THE *CLEAN* ARM: the gap-fill rows the judge never sees are entering the bibliography as LEAD citations, and they are garbage.** ARMED cites 3 unjudged fresh rows (ev_1283/1290/1291; CTRL cites 1). Bibliography entry **#1 = ev_1290**, whose ENTAILMENT_VERIFIED/SUPPORTS `claim_text` is a **chopped reference-list scrap** ("on and sustainable growth. _Technol. Forecast. Soc. Change_ 162, 120398..."); entry **#2 = ev_1291** has `basket_verdict: "unverified"` (weight 0.45) and is **cited in prose anyway**. So the clean report's two lead citations are junk that bypassed the judge via the Rank6a fold-in hole. My relevance metric is **blind to this** — it is a *quality* failure, not a topicality one, and unlike the alien-row problem **it actually shipped.** This is now the strongest known defect in the output.
98:  - **READABILITY REGRESSED (2.5 -> 2) on shipped ARTIFACTS**: **27 `(also mirrored)` markers leaked into prose** (CTRL: 3); **two sections open MID-WORD** — *"ings in the last two years.[85]"*, *"stionnaire developed by the researchers.[100][101]"*; and **fact_dedup leaked its own cross-reference into the report body**: *"The Brynjolfsson, Li, and Raymond customer-support agent study is detailed under Productivity Gains from Generative AI at Work.[97][98]"*
105:- 2026-07-13 00:25 | **DENSITY INSTRUMENT BUILT — AND IT CAUGHT ITS OWN BUG BEFORE I SHIPPED A NUMBER** (`scripts/flywheel_density.py`). Measures Fable's pre-registered falsifier: **distinct WORKS (not bib entries) per 1000 BODY words**, collapsing mirrors via the corpus's own `same_work_groups` -> DOI -> normalized title. **The bug: `report.md` ENDS WITH THE NUMBERED `## References` LIST**, so a marker regex over the whole file re-reads the bibliography as prose and reports **every entry as cited** — the tell was `cited == bib` for all four arms. Fixed to body-only (both numerator and denominator). **Corrected, it independently reproduces Fable's hand-count: 82 cited markers in rank7 (Fable counted 82), 77 distinct works (Fable estimated ~73).** Two independent methods converging is the strongest check available here. *This is the second measurement of mine tonight that returned a plausible number through a dead code path — the pattern is now the thing to watch, not the individual bug.*
116:  - **REINSTATED — AND IT WAS *MY* CAUTION THAT WAS WRONG:** I logged the CTRL->ARMED kept_fraction move (+0.085) as "~1.5σ, unresolved noise" using a **binomial SE of 0.055**. The empirical replicate says the true noise on kept_fraction is **~±0.01**, so +0.085 is **~8x the noise: Fable's prediction (|Δ|<=0.05) IS REFUTED.** My binomial model assumed sentence-level independence and **overestimated** the variance (Fable argued clustering would make it *bigger*; the measurement says smaller). **Both of us reasoned about noise for hours without ever measuring it. One replicate settled it in 50 minutes.** Caveat kept on the record: n=2 is a weak variance estimate, and the two runs share a judge seed and corpus, so they are not fully independent draws.

input.command: grep -o "(20[0-9][0-9])\|et al\.\|<sup>\|^[0-9]\+\." RACE_REFERENCE_task72.md | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head; echo ===; sed -n '25,40p' RACE_REFERENCE_task72.md; echo ===TAIL; tail -20 RACE_REFERENCE_task72.md

content.content:
3 et al.
===
### **2.1 The Task-Based Framework: Displacement and Reinstatement Dynamics**

A dominant theoretical lens for understanding technology's labor market impact is the task-based framework, notably developed and applied by Acemoglu and Restrepo. This framework posits that production processes consist of a collection of tasks, which are allocated to either labor or capital based on comparative advantage and cost-effectiveness. Technological change, particularly automation driven by AI and robotics, directly impacts this allocation.

The framework highlights two primary, opposing forces:

*   **Displacement Effect**: This occurs when automation technologies allow capital (machines, software, AI systems) to take over tasks previously performed by human workers. Automation directly substitutes capital for labor in these specific tasks, changing the task content of production adversely for labor. This displacement effect inherently reduces the demand for labor performing those tasks and tends to decrease the overall share of national income going to labor (the labor share). Examples range from industrial robots displacing workers on assembly lines to AI automating routine cognitive tasks in white-collar occupations or even non-routine tasks like driving.
*   **Reinstatement Effect**: Counterbalancing displacement is the potential for technology to create entirely *new tasks* in which labor holds a comparative advantage. The introduction of new products, processes, or industries often generates novel demands for human skills and capabilities. This reinstatement effect changes the task content of production in favor of labor, creating new job roles and responsibilities, thereby increasing labor demand and potentially boosting the labor share. Historical examples include new factory, clerical, engineering, and managerial roles emerging during previous industrial transformations. Contemporary examples might include roles in AI development, data science, AI ethics, or specialized human-AI interaction management.

Beyond these direct effects on task allocation, technology also generates a **Productivity Effect**. Both automation and the creation of new tasks can increase overall economic productivity, potentially leading to lower prices, higher output, and increased demand for labor across the economy, even in non-automated sectors. However, the magnitude of this effect can vary. Some research raises concerns about "so-so automation"—technologies that displace workers but offer only marginal productivity improvements, resulting in net negative impacts on labor demand.

The *net impact* of AI and automation on aggregate employment, wages, and the labor share ultimately depends on the dynamic balance between the displacement, reinstatement, and productivity effects. Some analyses suggest that in recent decades, the displacement effect associated with automation may have strengthened, while the reinstatement effect from new task creation has weakened compared to earlier periods of technological change, contributing to phenomena like wage stagnation and declining labor shares.

This framework underscores that the *nature* of technological advancement is critical. AI development primarily focused on substituting labor in existing tasks poses a greater inherent challenge to labor's economic standing than AI directed towards creating new tasks, augmenting human capabilities, or enabling entirely new industries. The historical stability of the labor share, despite continuous automation, relied heavily on the concurrent creation of new tasks. Concerns about "excessive" automation arise precisely when displacement occurs without strong countervailing reinstatement or productivity gains. This perspective suggests that the direction of AI innovation itself is a key determinant of labor market outcomes and potentially a target for policy influence, aiming to foster technologies that complement rather than solely replace human labor.

### **2.2 Skill-Biased, Task-Biased, and Routine-Biased Technical Change (RBTC)**
===TAIL

*   **Net Employment Outcome**: The most prominent disagreement concerns the ultimate net effect of AI on aggregate employment. Whether AI will primarily displace workers, leading to mass unemployment, or create sufficient new tasks and complementary roles to offset losses remains highly contested, with empirical evidence offering support for both pessimistic and optimistic arguments depending on the study.
*   **Pace and Scale of Disruption**: Relatedly, there is considerable uncertainty and debate about the speed at which AI's impacts will unfold and the overall magnitude of the resulting labor market disruption. Views range from imminent, rapid transformation to a more gradual evolution constrained by adoption lags and other factors.
*   **Magnitude of Wage Effects**: While automation is often linked theoretically to wage pressure and polarization, the empirical magnitude of these effects, particularly for AI as distinct from earlier automation, is still debated. Meta-analyses show complex and heterogeneous results for robot impacts, suggesting context matters greatly.
*   **Optimal Policy Mix**: While the need for policy intervention is widely acknowledged, significant debate surrounds the most effective and appropriate policy instruments. This is particularly true for taxation (e.g., the desirability and feasibility of automation taxes) and the appropriate balance between policies aimed at managing the transition (potentially slowing adoption) versus those aimed at accelerating innovation and productivity growth.

### **8.3 Identifying Research Gaps and Directions for Future Inquiry**

The reviewed literature also points towards several important gaps and promising directions for future research:

*   **Improved Measurement and Tracking**: There is a need for more granular, longitudinal data tracking the adoption of specific AI technologies (beyond just industrial robots) at the firm and establishment level, linked to worker-level outcomes over extended periods. Developing better metrics to capture AI diffusion and its economic effects, potentially addressing mismeasurement issues related to intangible capital and quality improvements, is also crucial.
*   **Understanding New Task Creation**: While the "displacement effect" is relatively well-studied, the "reinstatement effect" remains less understood. More research is needed on the processes through which AI leads to the creation of new tasks and occupations, the nature and skill requirements of these new jobs, and whether the pace of new task creation is sufficient to counterbalance automation-induced displacement.
*   **Generative AI Impacts**: Given the recent surge and distinct capabilities of generative AI, focused research is needed to understand its specific impacts on cognitive and creative work, intellectual property, skill demands, and potential for both productivity enhancement and displacement in previously less-affected occupations.
*   **Global and Contextual Diversity**: More empirical studies are required, particularly focusing on AI's impact in developing countries and across diverse institutional settings, moving beyond the concentration of research on developed economies.
*   **Market Structure and Inequality**: Further investigation into the interplay between AI adoption, firm dynamics (e.g., growth, market concentration, "superstar" effects), and the resulting impacts on income and wealth inequality is warranted.
*   **Effectiveness of Policies**: Rigorous evaluation of the effectiveness of different policy interventions—such as various reskilling program designs, alternative social safety net models, and specific tax or regulatory approaches—in mitigating negative impacts and facilitating adjustment is essential. Understanding the political economy of implementing such policies is also important.
*   **Dynamics of Adjustment**: A key overarching need is for research that moves beyond static comparisons to better understand the *dynamics* of the adjustment process. This includes studying the speed of firm adoption, the pathways workers take when transitioning between jobs or acquiring new skills, the real-time effectiveness of policy interventions, and the complex feedback loops between technological change, economic outcomes, and societal responses. Understanding these dynamic processes is critical for designing timely and effective strategies to navigate the AI-driven transformation of the labor market.
*   **Broader Societal Impacts**: Research should continue to explore the impacts of AI beyond the labor market, including ethical considerations, effects on worker well-being, privacy, social interaction, and democratic processes.

In conclusion, the literature confirms that AI is a powerful force reshaping labor markets, presenting both significant opportunities for productivity and innovation, and substantial challenges related to displacement, inequality, and the need for workforce adaptation. While theoretical frameworks provide valuable insights, empirical evidence remains complex and often context-dependent. Navigating this transformation successfully will require continued research to fill knowledge gaps, coupled with thoughtful, adaptive, and holistic policy responses aimed at harnessing AI's benefits while mitigating its risks and ensuring a prosperous and equitable future of work.

===LINE100
The pronounced heterogeneity in empirical findings across different studies and contexts strongly indicates that the labor market effects of AI and automation are not predetermined by the technology itself. Instead, they are heavily mediated by the specific economic structure, institutional environment, and policy choices of each country or region. This implies that policy interventions and local conditions play a critical role in shaping the ultimate consequences of technological change for workers.

Furthermore, a potential temporal lag and scale mismatch complicates the empirical assessment of AI's impact. While studies focusing on specific firms or tasks often detect clear signs of AI adoption and related changes in hiring or task requirements, detecting significant *aggregate* employment or wage effects specifically attributable to the recent surge in *AI* (as distinct from longer-term trends in robotics or broader automation) remains challenging in some large economies like the US. This contrasts with studies on industrial robots over longer periods, which did find measurable local aggregate effects. This discrepancy might be explained by implementation lags inherent to general-purp

content.text:
## Verdict: REFUTED

**1. The lever's own exemplar fails the lever's own gate.** It points at the reference's line ~100 (lags/scale). That paragraph actually reads: *"This discrepancy **might be explained by** implementation lags inherent to general-purpose technologies…"* — an abductive hypothesis, not something entailed by two facts. Same for the whole reference: `/home/polaris/polaris_project/RACE_REFERENCE_task72.md` has **0 citation markers, 0 bibliography, 3 total uses of "et al.", and 3 `%` signs in 9,026 words.** Its insight is *theory-driven*, not number-adjudicating. A rule that says "every interpretive claim must be entailed by ≥2 already-verified cited facts" structurally admits only the weakest genre — "study A found X, study B found Y, they differ by setting" — which **is** stated failure mode (1). The proposed mitigation ("name ≥2 specific cited findings") doesn't prevent generic comparison prose; it *mandates* it. Meanwhile insight's two biggest sub-criteria — Mechanisms (0.25) and Emergent Themes/Theoretical Linkages (0.20), 0.45 of 0.32 — are exactly the claims that are *never* entailed by two empirical facts. The gate forbids the payload.

**2. The diagnostic behind the claimed gain is a broken measurement.** "The reference's ~13 citation-free interpretive paragraphs are the single largest measured difference" — the reference is **100% citation-free**, so "citation-free" cannot discriminate anything; whatever produced "13" measured something else. The actually-largest structural differences are unattended: reference has **24 H3 subsections + 76 bolded taxonomy bullets + a "Table 1: Summary of Key Empirical Studies"**; our report is **11 flat H2s whose bodies are ONE paragraph each of 527–984 words** (verified: `report.md` sections are single lines). Single-cause attribution of a 0.076 insight gap to "13 paragraphs" across a doc that differs on 5+ axes is unsupported.

**3. The repo has already run this experiment and it backfired.** `FLYWHEEL_PROGRESS.md:94,100`: Rank7 doubled depth/length (3,408→7,723w) and RACE moved **insight 2.5→3 only, while readability regressed 2.5→2**, and — logged verbatim — *"distinct-works-per-1000-words FELL 11.8 → 9.5. I bought length by lowering insight density — and **RACE grades density, not totals**."* The argue pass adds ~2.5–3k words containing **zero new works**: density mechanically drops ~25% (88 works / 9.2k → 88 / 11.7k). That is the exact vector that already failed.

**4. Backfire, both branches of the length fork:**
- *Append (no cut):* density falls (above); readability P1 explicitly scores "avoidance of redundancy," and the same Humlum-vs-RCT tension now gets argued **three times** — in the per-section argue pass, in the **already-existing** `## Cross-Study Synthesis and Contradictions` section, and again in the rewritten conclusion. The lever never says to delete the existing synthesis section.
- *Fixed 9k:* 20 paragraphs ≈ 30% of the body displaces facts. Comprehensiveness's coverage-shaped subs (breadth .25 + industry scope .25 + literature representativeness .15 = 0.65 of w=0.29) are precisely what dies. And the funding is explicitly deferred to "the padding cut (next lever)" — so the claimed +0.021 is **not independent**; crediting both levers double-spends the same word budget.

**5. Document self-contradiction (judge-visible).** `report.md` Limitations currently says *"No contradictions were detected by the pipeline, so no cross-source disagreement on magnitude, direction, or endpoint is asserted,"* and the intro promises *"Every quantitative claim is span-grounded."* An entailment-exempt pass whose whole job is asserting cross-source disagreement makes both sentences false → IF (literature-review coherence) and readability hits.

**6. Ceiling arithmetic.** +0.021 requires insight 0.424 → 0.49, i.e. closing ~2/3 of the gap to a reference that is *definitionally* 0.5. With only 0.70 of insight weight touched, those three subs must each gain ~+0.09 (≈ a full judge band) from prose adding **no new evidence** and restricted to inter-number comparison. Not credible.

Files: `/home/polaris/wt/flywheel/outputs/rank10_sections_compose/report.md`, `/home/polaris/polaris_project/RACE_REFERENCE_task72.md`, `/home/polaris/polaris_project/FLYWHEEL_PROGRESS.md` (lines 94, 100), `/home/polaris/polaris_project/race_task72_criteria.txt`.

input.lever: Synthesis layer: an 'argue' pass exempt from span-grounding
input.reasoning:
REFUTED on six concrete grounds.

(1) SELF-DEFEATING GATE. The lever's own exemplar — the reference's line ~100 lags/scale passage — reads "This discrepancy MIGHT BE EXPLAINED BY implementation lags inherent to general-purpose technologies". That is an abductive hypothesis, NOT a claim entailed by two verified facts. It would be rejected by the lever's own verification rule. Generalizing: insight's two heaviest sub-criteria — Analytical Depth in Mechanisms (0.25) and Emergent Themes/Theoretical Linkages (0.20) = 0.45 of the 0.32 dim — are by nature conceptual/abductive and are essentially NEVER entailed by two empirical numbers. The "entailed by >=2 verified facts" rule therefore admits only the weakest genre ("A found X, B found Y, they differ by setting/time horizon") — which IS the lever's own stated failure mode (1). The proposed mitigation ("name >=2 specific cited findings") does not prevent generic comparison prose; it mandates it.

(2) THE DIAGNOSTIC IS A BROKEN MEASUREMENT. Claim: "the reference's ~13 citation-free interpretive paragraphs are the single largest measured difference." Verified: RACE_REFERENCE_task72.md has 0 bracket citation markers, NO bibliography at all, 3 total uses of "et al.", and 3 percent-signs in 9,026 words. The reference is 100% citation-free, so "citation-free" cannot discriminate 13 paragraphs from the rest; whatever counter produced "13" measured something else. The genuinely largest structural deltas are unaddressed: reference = 24 H3 subsections + 76 bolded taxonomy bullets + "Table 1: Summary of Key Empirical Studies"; ours = 11 flat H2s whose bodies are ONE paragraph each of 527-984 words. Attributing a 0.076 insight gap to "13 paragraphs" across a doc differing on 5+ axes is unproven.

(3) THE REPO ALREADY RAN THIS AND IT BACKFIRED. FLYWHEEL_PROGRESS.md:100 — Rank7 depth/length doubling (3,408 -> 7,723w) moved RACE insight only 2.5 -> 3 while READABILITY REGRESSED 2.5 -> 2. Line 94, verbatim: "distinct-works-per-1000-words FELL 11.8 -> 9.5. I bought length by lowering insight density — and RACE grades density, not totals." The argue pass adds ~2.5-3k words containing ZERO new works: density drops ~25% (88 works/9.2k -> 88/11.7k). Same failed vector.

(4) BACKFIRE ON BOTH FORKS OF THE LENGTH DECISION. Append: density falls (above) and redundancy explodes — the Humlum-vs-RCT tension gets argued three times (per-section argue pass + the ALREADY EXISTING "## Cross-Study Synthesis and Contradictions" section + the rewritten conclusion); readability P1 explicitly scores "avoidance of redundancy", and the lever never says to delete the existing synthesis section. Fixed 9k: ~20 paragraphs = ~30% of the body displaces facts, and comprehensiveness's coverage-shaped subs (breadth .25 + industry scope .25 + literature representativeness .15 = 0.65 of w=0.29) are exactly what dies. Worse, the funding is explicitly deferred to "the padding cut (next lever)" — so the +0.021 is NOT independent; crediting both levers double-spends the same word budget.

(5) SELF-CONTRADICTING DOCUMENT. report.md's Limitations currently asserts "No contradictions were detected by the pipeline, so no cross-source disagreement on magnitude, direction, or endpoint is asserted", and the intro promises "Every quantitative claim is span-grounded". An entailment-exempt pass whose job is asserting cross-source disagreement falsifies both — a judge-visible coherence defect hitting instruction_following (lit-review coherence, w=0.25) and readability.

(6) CEILING ARITHMETIC. +0.021 needs insight 0.424 -> 0.49, i.e. closing ~2/3 of the gap to a reference that is 0.5 BY DEFINITION. Only 0.70 of insight weight is touched, so those subs must each rise ~+0.09 (about a full judge band) on prose that adds no new evidence and is restricted by its own gate to inter-number comparison. Not credible.
input.salvage:
Three cheap, low-risk pieces survive; the expensive per-section argue pass does not.

(a) THE CONCLUSION REWRITE (consensus -> disagreements -> named research gaps). ~500 words, mirrors the reference's own 8.1/8.2/8.3 structure verbatim, and hits insight's "Value and Foresight in Delineating Implications and Future Research Agendas" (0.15) plus readability S1 ("a conclusion that synthesizes main themes and points to gaps"). Keep it. It does not need the entailment gate.

(b) THE INTRO THESIS/ROADMAP PARAGRAPH. Our report's Introduction literally opens mid-thought ("Within this framework, automation generates a displacement effect...") with no antecedent — a flat defect. A framing + organizational-map paragraph fixes readability S1 and IF "literature review format" for near-zero cost. Keep it.

(c) FOLD, DON'T FAN OUT. Put any argue output INTO the existing "Cross-Study Synthesis and Contradictions" section (currently starved and full of raw facts, not contradictions) instead of adding 2-3 commentary paragraphs to each of 8 sections. One location = no triplication, no ~3k-word blowout, no density collapse.

(d) DROP the ">=2 verified facts entailment" definition of insight. The reference's insight is abductive/theoretical (explicitly hedged: "might be explained by"). Allow bounded, explicitly-hedged mechanism hypotheses; the safety property you want is hedging, not entailment.

(e) REDIRECT the effort at the structural deltas this lever mis-attributes and which are cheaper and hit three dimensions at once: H3 subsections (ours: 11 x one 500-1000-word blob paragraph), bolded mechanism-taxonomy bullets (reference has 76), and a Table 1 summary-of-key-studies (readability D1 rewards this explicitly; comprehensiveness benefits too). These raise insight legibility WITHOUT lowering works-per-1000-words.
